title: Endocrine Disorders of Critical Illness
authors: Jan Gunst and Greet Van den Berghe

items.title: Why endocrine alterations differ in acute versus prolonged critical illness
items.text: Characteristic endocrine changes in critical illness differ between the acute and prolonged phases and complicate diagnosis of preexisting endocrine disease.
items.reference: KEY POINTS: "In critical illness, characteristic endocrine alterations develop; these alterations differ in the acute versus prolonged phase of illness and complicate the diagnosis of preexisting endocrine disease."

items.title: How the acute critical illness endocrine phenotype looks
items.text: Acute critical illness features low thyroid hormone and IGF-1 with high cortisol—presumably adaptive changes driven mainly by peripheral hormone metabolism.
items.reference: KEY POINTS: "Acute critical illness is characterized by low levels of thyroid hormone and insulin-like growth factor 1, and high levels of cortisol, which is presumably adaptive and mainly brought about by peripheral alterations in hormone metabolism."

items.title: Why prolonged ICU stay causes central neuroendocrine suppression
items.text: In prolonged critical illness, central suppression of somatotropic, thyrotropic, and adrenocorticotropic axes may impair rehabilitation; optimal treatment awaits adequately powered trials.
items.reference: KEY POINTS: "In patients with prolonged critical illness, a central suppression of the somatotropic, thyrotropic, and adrenocorticotropic axes progressively develops, which may impair rehabilitation, although treatment indications and regimens remain to be studied in adequately powered trials."

items.title: How to achieve safe blood glucose control in the ICU
items.text: Safe glycemic control requires a validated protocol with accurate frequent glucose measurements and avoidance of insulin boluses.
items.reference: KEY POINTS: "Safe blood glucose control in critically ill patients requires a validated protocol, with accurate and frequent blood glucose measurements and avoidance of insulin boluses."

items.title: Why the ideal ICU glucose target remains unclear
items.text: Without early parenteral nutrition, the optimal blood glucose target in critically ill patients has not been definitively established.
items.reference: KEY POINTS: "The ideal blood glucose target in critically ill patients not receiving early parenteral nutrition remains unclear."

items.title: How to interpret low vitamin D in acute critical illness
items.text: Low vitamin D concentrations in critically ill patients are probably adaptive during the acute phase of illness.
items.reference: KEY POINTS: "Critically ill patients have low vitamin D concentrations, which is probably adaptive in the acute phase of illness."

items.title: Why critical illness triggers profound endocrine alterations
items.text: Severe illness, major surgery, or trauma provoke neuroendocrine changes, insulin resistance, stress hyperglycemia, and vitamin D alterations assumed adaptive but complicating preexisting endocrine diagnosis.
items.reference: Introduction: "As part of the stress response, profound endocrine alterations occur, including neuroendocrine changes, insulin resistance and stress hyperglycemia, and alterations in vitamin D, which have been assumed to be adaptive."

items.title: How the GH axis changes in acute critical illness
items.text: GH secretion increases with higher pulse frequency and amplitude, but peripheral GH resistance lowers hepatic receptors and IGF-1, IGFBP3, and ALS.
items.reference: Alterations in the Somatotropic Axis: "In the acute phase of critical illness, growth hormone (GH) secretion increases, with increased pulse frequency and amplitude and elevated trough concentrations."

items.title: Why high-dose GH increases mortality in prolonged critical illness
items.text: RCTs showed high-dose GH after 5–7 days in expected long-stay ICU patients significantly increased mortality, likely from GH overdosing when peripheral responsiveness recovers.
items.reference: Alterations in the Somatotropic Axis: "two randomized controlled trials (RCTs) revealed that high-dose GH treatment, initiated after 5 to 7 days in expected long-stay critically ill patients, significantly increased mortality, which may be explained by GH overdosing in a state of recovered GH responsiveness."

items.title: How dopamine suppresses the somatotropic axis in ICU
items.text: Dopamine, historically used as a vasoactive agent, centrally suppresses the somatotropic axis in critically ill patients.
items.reference: Alterations in the Somatotropic Axis: "The administration of dopamine treatment, commonly used as vasoactive strategy in the past, centrally suppresses the somatotropic axis."

items.title: Why T3 falls rapidly in acute critical illness
items.text: Type 1 deiodinase activity falls while type 3 deiodinase rises, increasing T4 conversion to inactive rT3 and T3 degradation, lowering T3 and the T3:rT3 ratio.
items.reference: Alterations in the Thyroid Axis: "Mechanistically, the activity of type 1 deiodinase is attenuated while type 3 deiodinase activity increases, leading to increased peripheral conversion of thyroxine ( $ T_{4} $) secreted by the thyroid gland to the biologically inactive reverse  $ T_{3} $ ( $ rT_{3} $)."

items.title: How euthyroid sick syndrome presents biochemically
items.text: Low T3 with brief TSH and T4 rise then normalization defines euthyroid sick syndrome, nonthyroid illness, or acute low T3 syndrome triggered by inflammation and fasting.
items.reference: Alterations in the Thyroid Axis: "These alterations have been designated as euthyroid sick syndrome, nonthyroid illness, or acute low  $ T_{3} $ syndrome."

items.title: Why low or normal TSH does not exclude hypothyroidism in ICU
items.text: In overt primary hypothyroidism during critical illness, TSH may be lower than expected; history, exam, autoantibodies, and repeat testing after recovery aid diagnosis.
items.reference: Alterations in the Thyroid Axis: "In critically ill patients who do have overt primary hypothyroidism, the TSH concentrations may be lower than expected. Hence, a low or normal TSH does not exclude primary hypothyroidism."

items.title: How ICU drugs suppress the thyroid axis
items.text: Iatrogenic suppression arises from iodine dressings, contrast, glucocorticoids, somatostatin, dopamine, and amiodarone.
items.reference: Alterations in the Thyroid Axis: "Apart from the critical illness-induced alterations, TSH secretion and/or thyroid hormone production may be suppressed by iatrogenic factors, including iodine-containing wound dressings and contrast agents, and drugs such as glucocorticoids, somatostatin, dopamine, and amiodarone."

items.title: Why ACTH-cortisol dissociation occurs after acute stress
items.text: After brief ACTH rise, ACTH falls while cortisol stays high because cortisol breakdown is suppressed and binding protein changes increase free cortisol.
items.reference: Alterations in the Hypothalamic-Pituitary-Adrenal Axis: "The persistent hypercortisolemia in the presence of suppressed ACTH (ACTH-cortisol dissociation) after the hyperacute phase is explained by suppressed breakdown of cortisol."

items.title: How CRH and vasopressin relate to ACTH suppression
items.text: Negative feedback from hypercortisolemia suppresses pituitary ACTH while ongoing CRH and arginine-vasopressin drive POMC expression without ACTH processing.
items.reference: Alterations in the Hypothalamic-Pituitary-Adrenal Axis: "Recent evidence indicates that the rapid suppression of ACTH following acute stress is explained by negative feedback inhibition through stress-induced hypercortisolemia on the pituitary gland, while ongoing stress-induced hypothalamic stimulation through corticotropin-releasing hormone (CRH) and arginine-vasopressin (AVP) leads to ongoing expression of the ACTH precursor pro-opiomelanocortin (POMC), which is no longer processed into ACTH."

items.title: Why adrenal insufficiency is hard to diagnose in critical illness
items.text: Elevated cortisol largely reflects peripheral adaptations, not increased production, so random cortisol and cosyntropin testing are difficult to interpret.
items.reference: Alterations in the Hypothalamic-Pituitary-Adrenal Axis: "the diagnosis of adrenal insufficiency in critical illness is very difficult, because high cortisol concentrations are largely mediated by peripheral adaptations and not by increased cortisol production."

items.title: How septic shock steroid trials inform CIRCI controversy
items.text: Large RCTs showed no mortality benefit from hydrocortisone in low cosyntropin responders; recent evidence questions CIRCI and generalized glucocorticoid resistance concepts.
items.reference: Alterations in the Hypothalamic-Pituitary-Adrenal Axis: "Altogether, recent evidence questions the traditional concept of critical illness-related corticosteroid insufficiency (CIRCI), which has been assumed to be present in septic shock, and has been related to a decreased incremental cortisol response to ACTH, as well as to generalized glucocorticoid resistance."

items.title: Why stress hyperglycemia develops in critical illness
items.text: Severe inflammation and counter-regulatory hormone elevations usually cause hyperglycemia associated with poor ICU outcomes.
items.reference: Insulin Resistance and Stress Hyperglycemia: "In response to severe inflammation and elevations in counter-regulatory hormones, critically ill patients usually develop hyperglycemia, which has been associated with poor outcomes."

items.title: How Leuven and NICE-SUGAR trials differ in glucose control
items.text: Discordant RCT results reflect different control-group targets, monitoring accuracy, insulin protocols, and feeding strategies—notably early parenteral nutrition in Leuven.
items.reference: Insulin Resistance and Stress Hyperglycemia: "The outcome differences between these landmark RCTs have been attributed to differences in glucose targets in the control group, differences in accuracy of the glucose monitoring and the insulin protocol, and differences in feeding strategies (Table 48.1)."

items.title: Why insulin boluses should be avoided in ICU glucose control
items.text: Continuous IV insulin infusion without boluses slowly reaches target and minimizes hypoglycemia and glucose variability, both linked to poor outcomes.
items.reference: Insulin Resistance and Stress Hyperglycemia: "Insulin is preferably administered through continuous intravenous insulin infusion while avoiding boluses, to slowly progress toward blood glucose target and to avoid hypoglycemia and large glucose fluctuations, because both have been associated with poor outcomes."

items.title: How HbA1c distinguishes stress hyperglycemia from diabetes
items.text: Routine admission HbA1c helps separate pure stress hyperglycemia from hyperglycemia superimposed on previously unknown diabetes, guiding post-ICU follow-up.
items.reference: Insulin Resistance and Stress Hyperglycemia: "Therefore, routine HbA $ _{1c} $ measurements may aid in discriminating pure stress hyperglycemia versus stress hyperglycemia superimposed on diabetes mellitus, which could be important for post-ICU follow-up."

items.title: Why prolonged critical illness increases bone resorption risk
items.text: Prolonged ICU patients show increased bone resorption and fracture risk, potentially mediated partly by vitamin D deficiency.
items.reference: Alterations in Vitamin D: "Prolonged critically ill patients have signs of increased bone resorption and increased fracture risk."

items.title: How vitamin D supplementation RCTs inform ICU practice
items.text: High-dose vitamin D RCTs raised 25OHD or active hormone without clinical benefit; acute low vitamin D is likely adaptive and needs no pharmacologic correction beyond maintenance dose.
items.reference: Alterations in Vitamin D: "Altogether, current evidence suggests that low vitamin D concentrations are adaptive during acute critical illness and do not require pharmacologic correction on top of a normal maintenance dose."

items.title: Why prolonged critical illness endocrine profile may be maladaptive
items.text: Patients remaining ICU-dependent for weeks develop a different endocrine profile that may contribute to hypercatabolism and immune suppression.
items.reference: Conclusion: "In patients who do not recover swiftly and remain dependent on intensive care for a prolonged period of time, a different endocrine profile develops, which may be maladaptive by contributing to the hypercatabolic phenotype and immune suppression associated with this condition."

items.title: How illness-induced endocrine changes impede diagnosis
items.text: Neuroendocrine abnormalities, stress hyperglycemia, and low vitamin D proportional to illness severity often obscure preexisting endocrine disease in the ICU.
items.reference: Conclusion: "The illness-induced alterations often impede the diagnosis of preexisting endocrine disease."

items.question: A septic ICU patient has low T3, normal-low TSH, and no hypothyroid symptoms. Most appropriate interpretation?
options: Euthyroid sick (nonthyroidal illness) syndrome
Primary hypothyroidism requiring immediate levothyroxine
TSH-secreting pituitary adenoma
Factitious thyrotoxicosis from exogenous T3
items.explanation: Low T3 with normalized TSH/T4 defines euthyroid sick syndrome triggered by inflammation and fasting in acute critical illness.
items.reference: Alterations in the Thyroid Axis: "These alterations have been designated as euthyroid sick syndrome, nonthyroid illness, or acute low  $ T_{3} $ syndrome."

items.question: A mechanically ventilated patient on amiodarone and dopamine has low TSH and low T4. Besides critical illness, what should be considered?
options: Iatrogenic suppression from ICU medications
Isolated primary hyperthyroidism
Thyroid storm requiring emergent thyroidectomy
Pituitary macroadenoma is the only explanation
items.explanation: Glucocorticoids, somatostatin, dopamine, amiodarone, and iodine sources can suppress TSH and thyroid hormone production in ICU patients.
items.reference: Alterations in the Thyroid Axis: "Apart from the critical illness-induced alterations, TSH secretion and/or thyroid hormone production may be suppressed by iatrogenic factors, including iodine-containing wound dressings and contrast agents, and drugs such as glucocorticoids, somatostatin, dopamine, and amiodarone."

items.question: An ICU patient with known hypothyroidism has low-normal TSH. Can primary hypothyroidism still be present?
options: Yes—a low or normal TSH does not exclude primary hypothyroidism
No—normal TSH always rules out hypothyroidism in ICU
Only if TSH is above 20 mIU/L
Only in hyperthyroid patients on methimazole
items.explanation: Critical illness lowers expected TSH in overt primary hypothyroidism; diagnosis requires clinical context and repeat testing after recovery.
items.reference: Alterations in the Thyroid Axis: "In critically ill patients who do have overt primary hypothyroidism, the TSH concentrations may be lower than expected. Hence, a low or normal TSH does not exclude primary hypothyroidism."

items.question: A critically ill patient has unexpectedly elevated T3. Best next step in interpretation?
options: Suspect hyperthyroidism or thyroid storm
Dismiss as adaptive euthyroid sick syndrome
Start high-dose levothyroxine immediately
Attribute solely to fasting without further workup
items.explanation: Elevated T3 is highly unusual in critical illness and should prompt suspicion of hyperthyroidism or thyroid storm.
items.reference: Alterations in the Thyroid Axis: "Conversely, elevated  $ T_{3} $ concentrations are highly unusual in critical illness and should always lead to suspicion of hyperthyroidism or even thyroid storm."

items.question: A medical ICU team considers levothyroxine for low T3 in acute sepsis without hypothyroidism. Evidence-based approach?
options: Do not supplement—acute alterations are adaptive without outcome benefit
Always give T4 plus T3 to all septic patients
Supplement only when TSH exceeds 20 mIU/L in acute sepsis
Thyroid hormone always improves mortality in acute critical illness
items.explanation: Acute-phase thyroid alterations are assumed adaptive; interventional thyroid hormone supplementation has not shown outcome benefit.
items.reference: Alterations in the Thyroid Axis: "In the acute phase of critical illness, these alterations are assumed to be adaptive. $ ^{2} $ Interventional studies supplementing thyroid hormone in the acute phase of critical illness have not shown benefit on outcomes."

items.question: A patient with septic shock has total cortisol 28 µg/dL and low ACTH. Does this exclude adrenal insufficiency?
options: No—elevated cortisol does not exclude adrenal insufficiency in critical illness
Yes—any cortisol above 15 µg/dL rules out insufficiency
Only if ACTH is simultaneously elevated
Cortisol is uninterpretable only in hypothyroidism
items.explanation: High cortisol in critical illness largely reflects peripheral adaptations; random cortisol is difficult to interpret for adrenal insufficiency.
items.reference: Alterations in the Hypothalamic-Pituitary-Adrenal Axis: "Hence, random cortisol measurements can be difficult to interpret because elevated cortisol concentrations do not exclude adrenal insufficiency."

items.question: An ICU fellow plans cosyntropin stimulation to decide hydrocortisone therapy in septic shock. Best evidence-based advice?
options: Cosyntropin test does not identify patients who benefit from glucocorticoids
Low cosyntropin response mandates high-dose dexamethasone for mortality benefit
Cosyntropin response perfectly predicts steroid-responsive shock
Only free T4 guides steroid decisions in sepsis
items.explanation: Although low incremental cortisol response correlated with mortality observationally, large RCTs showed no mortality impact from steroids in low responders.
items.reference: Alterations in the Hypothalamic-Pituitary-Adrenal Axis: "The cosyntropin stimulation test does not identify patients who would benefit from treatment with glucocorticoids."

items.question: A long-stay ICU patient weaned from vasopressors develops hypotension and hyponatremia one week after ICU discharge with rising ACTH and cortisol. Likely mechanism?
options: Reversible central HPA suppression unmasked after ICU discharge
Primary hyperaldosteronism from exogenous fludrocortisone only
Isolated SIADH unrelated to HPA axis
Thyroid storm causing secondary adrenal failure
items.explanation: Central HPA suppression in prolonged illness may manifest after discharge; ACTH and cortisol rise to supranormal levels corroborating reversible central suppression.
items.reference: Alterations in the Hypothalamic-Pituitary-Adrenal Axis: "On the other hand, the central suppression of the HPA axis observed in long-stay ICU patients, and the rise of ACTH and cortisol 1 week after ICU discharge, suggest that central adrenal insufficiency may exist in a subset of long-stay ICU patients, which could clinically manifest as a persistent need for vasopressors, impaired consciousness, and electrolyte abnormalities."

items.question: Regarding hydrocortisone in septic shock, which statement matches recent RCT evidence?
options: Most trials show faster vasopressor weaning but inconsistent mortality benefit
All RCTs show uniform 50% mortality reduction
Steroids never shorten vasopressor duration
Hydrocortisone is contraindicated in all septic shock
items.explanation: Hydrocortisone RCTs often shortened vasopressor support but showed inconsistent benefit on hard endpoints; only one RCT found significant mortality benefit.
items.reference: Alterations in the Hypothalamic-Pituitary-Adrenal Axis: "Although most RCTs demonstrated faster weaning of vasopressor support, which could be expected based on the well-known blood pressure-augmenting effects of steroids, only one RCT found significant mortality benefit."

items.question: A prolonged ICU patient has low IGF-1 with declining GH pulses. Consultant considers GH therapy day 6. Best recommendation?
options: Avoid high-dose GH—RCTs increased mortality in long-stay critically ill patients
Start high-dose GH to reverse catabolism immediately
GH is first-line anabolic therapy after day 3 in all ICU patients
GH only contraindicated in children not adults
items.explanation: High-dose GH initiated after 5–7 days in expected long-stay patients significantly increased mortality in RCTs.
items.reference: Alterations in the Somatotropic Axis: "two randomized controlled trials (RCTs) revealed that high-dose GH treatment, initiated after 5 to 7 days in expected long-stay critically ill patients, significantly increased mortality, which may be explained by GH overdosing in a state of recovered GH responsiveness."

items.question: An ICU patient on dopamine infusion has low IGF-1. Besides critical illness itself, what drug effect should be considered?
options: Dopamine centrally suppresses the somatotropic axis
Dopamine exclusively stimulates GH secretion
Dopamine has no pituitary effects
Dopamine only affects thyroid hormone binding globulin
items.explanation: Dopamine treatment centrally suppresses the somatotropic axis among ICU therapeutic effects on endocrine axes.
items.reference: Alterations in the Somatotropic Axis: "The administration of dopamine treatment, commonly used as vasoactive strategy in the past, centrally suppresses the somatotropic axis."

items.question: In acute critical illness, GH is high but IGF-1 is low. Primary mechanism?
options: Peripheral GH resistance with low hepatic GH receptors
Primary pituitary GH deficiency
Excess IGF-1 feedback suppressing GH
Isolated renal failure clearing all GH
items.explanation: Acute illness combines increased GH secretion with peripheral GH resistance lowering hepatic receptors and IGF-1, IGFBP3, and ALS.
items.reference: Alterations in the Somatotropic Axis: "Similarly, peripheral growth hormone resistance occurs, with lowered expression of hepatic GH receptors, leading to low concentrations of insulin-like growth factor 1 (IGF1), IGF-binding protein 3 (IGFBP3), and acid-labile subunit (ALS) (Fig. 48.1)."

items.question: A surgical ICU uses tight glucose control 80–110 mg/dL with insulin boluses and capillary glucometers, no early PN. Compared with Leuven protocols, concern?
options: NICE-SUGAR showed harm with tight control; boluses and capillary monitoring increase risk
Identical safety to Leuven arterial monitoring without boluses
Tight control is always beneficial regardless of feeding or monitoring
Insulin boluses are preferred to continuous infusion in ICU
items.explanation: NICE-SUGAR showed harm from tight control vs <180 mg/dL; differences include bolus insulin, less accurate monitoring, and no early PN context of Leuven benefit.
items.reference: Insulin Resistance and Stress Hyperglycemia: "the NICE-SUGAR RCT subsequently showed harm by tight glucose control compared with maintaining blood glucose below 180 mg/dL."

items.question: An ICU nurse asks preferred site for glucose monitoring during IV glucose infusion. Best answer?
options: Arterial blood glucose measurements are preferred
Capillary glucometer always preferred in shock
Venous samples are never falsely elevated
Urinary glucose replaces blood testing in ICU
items.explanation: Arterial glucose is preferred; venous values may be falsely high with concurrent IV glucose, and capillary values are unreliable with poor perfusion.
items.reference: Insulin Resistance and Stress Hyperglycemia: "Arterial blood glucose measurements are preferred, because venous measurements can be falsely elevated in cases of concurrent intravenous glucose infusion, and capillary measurements are potentially unreliable in"

items.question: A diabetic ICU patient with admission HbA1c 10.2% develops glucose 95 mg/dL on insulin infusion. Special vulnerability?
options: Poorly controlled diabetics are more vulnerable to severe and relative hypoglycemia
Diabetics are immune to hypoglycemia in critical illness
HbA1c has no bearing on hypoglycemia risk in ICU
Only nondiabetic patients suffer hypoglycemia-related harm
items.explanation: Observational data show poorly controlled diabetes increases vulnerability to severe and relative hypoglycemia during critical illness.
items.reference: Insulin Resistance and Stress Hyperglycemia: "Patients with poorly controlled diabetes may be more vulnerable to severe and relative hypoglycemia, $ ^{67,68} $"

items.question: A patient without known diabetes has ICU glucose 240 mg/dL and HbA1c 7.8% on admission. Clinical implication?
options: Likely stress hyperglycemia superimposed on previously unknown diabetes
Pure stress hyperglycemia—HbA1c is never useful in ICU
HbA1c only reflects the last 24 hours of glucose
Admission hyperglycemia never warrants post-ICU endocrine follow-up
items.explanation: Routine HbA1c helps discriminate pure stress hyperglycemia from hyperglycemia on underlying diabetes, informing post-ICU follow-up.
items.reference: Insulin Resistance and Stress Hyperglycemia: "Therefore, routine HbA $ _{1c} $ measurements may aid in discriminating pure stress hyperglycemia versus stress hyperglycemia superimposed on diabetes mellitus, which could be important for post-ICU follow-up."

items.question: When is tight glucose control benefit most clearly demonstrated per chapter evidence?
options: With early parenteral nutrition supplementing insufficient enteral feeds
Only when insulin boluses are used liberally
Exclusively in patients not receiving any nutrition
Never in any ICU population under any protocol
items.explanation: Tight glucose control benefit was demonstrated in the context of early parenteral nutrition, a strategy later abandoned because of harm.
items.reference: Insulin Resistance and Stress Hyperglycemia: "In this context, a benefit of tight glucose control was only demonstrated in a context of early parenteral nutrition supplementing insufficient enteral nutrition, $ ^{10-12,61} $"

items.question: An ICU patient has low 25OHD on admission day 1. Acute management per current evidence?
options: Low vitamin D is likely adaptive—no pharmacologic correction beyond maintenance dose
Immediate megadose vitamin D3 to normalize all metabolites acutely
Stop all calcium and vitamin D in every ICU patient
IV 25OHD always normalizes active 1,25(OH)2D in acute illness
items.explanation: Current evidence suggests acute low vitamin D is adaptive and does not require pharmacologic correction on top of normal maintenance dosing.
items.reference: Alterations in Vitamin D: "Altogether, current evidence suggests that low vitamin D concentrations are adaptive during acute critical illness and do not require pharmacologic correction on top of a normal maintenance dose."

items.question: A 6-week ICU survivor is evaluated for bone health. Finding associated with prolonged critical illness?
options: Increased bone resorption and increased fracture risk
Osteoporosis is impossible during critical illness
Vitamin D always normalizes active hormone with oral D3 in ICU
Fracture risk decreases during prolonged ICU stay
items.explanation: Prolonged critically ill patients demonstrate increased bone resorption and fracture risk, potentially linked to vitamin D alterations.
items.reference: Alterations in Vitamin D: "Prolonged critically ill patients have signs of increased bone resorption and increased fracture risk."

items.question: A patient with preexisting hypothyroidism on oral levothyroxine is NPO and needs IV replacement. Dose adjustment?
options: Reduce substitution dose by approximately 25% for IV therapy
Double the oral dose when switching to IV
Stop all thyroid hormone during any ICU admission
IV levothyroxine requires no dose change from oral
items.explanation: IV substitution is usually reduced ~25% to account for roughly 75% oral levothyroxine bioavailability.
items.reference: Alterations in the Thyroid Axis: "When the patient is in need of intravenous substitution, the substitution dose is usually reduced by approximately 25%, to account for an oral bioavailability of roughly 75%."

items.question: Stress-dose hydrocortisone in septic shock without adrenal insufficiency compared with endogenous cortisol production?
options: Stress doses far exceed estimated ~60 mg/day cortisol production rate
Stress doses are always below physiologic cortisol production
Endogenous production exceeds 500 mg/day in all septic patients
Hydrocortisone has no hemodynamic effects in shock
items.explanation: Stress hydrocortisone doses for septic shock exceed the estimated ~60 mg/day cortisol production in hyperinflammatory patients without adrenal insufficiency.
items.reference: Alterations in the Hypothalamic-Pituitary-Adrenal Axis: "Indeed, stress doses of hydrocortisone to prevent adrenal crisis and as adjunctive treatment of septic shock far exceed the cortisol production rate in hyperinflammatory patients without adrenal insufficiency, which has been estimated to be approximately 60 mg per day."

items.question: Tight insulin therapy in fed critically ill adults affects the GH axis how?
options: Increases GH resistance with lower IGF-1, IGFBP3, and ALS and higher GH
Normalizes IGF-1 by suppressing all GH secretion permanently
Has no effect on somatotropic hormones
Eliminates need for glucose monitoring
items.explanation: Tight glucose control with insulin in a fed state increases GH resistance, suppressing IGF-1 and binding proteins while raising GH.
items.reference: Alterations in the Somatotropic Axis: "by intensive insulin therapy in a fed state further increases GH resistance in both critically ill children and adults, with suppression of IGF1 and the ternary complex proteins IGFBP3 and ALS, and results in a concomitant increase in GH."

items.question: Prolonged ICU patient has very low T3, low TSH, and low T4. Central mechanism?
options: Hyposecretion of TRH with absent pulsatile TSH secretion
Isolated primary thyroid gland destruction only
Excess dietary iodine is the sole cause
Pituitary TSH hypersecretion from thyroid resistance
items.explanation: Prolonged illness further lowers T3 via virtually absent pulsatile TSH from hypothalamic TRH hyposecretion.
items.reference: Alterations in the Thyroid Axis: "In prolonged critical illness, alterations in peripheral metabolism often persist:  $ T_3 $ concentrations further decline due to the virtually absent pulsatile TSH secretion, and this results in low TSH and  $ T_4 $ concentrations."

items.question: An ICU director implements a glucose protocol. Minimum safety elements per chapter?
options: Validated protocol, accurate frequent measurements, no insulin boluses
Capillary checks once daily with prn boluses
Target <70 mg/dL in all adults regardless of diabetes
Oral hypoglycemics as first-line in ventilated patients
items.explanation: KEY POINTS mandate validated protocol, accurate frequent glucose measurement, and avoidance of insulin boluses for safe ICU glycemic control.
items.reference: KEY POINTS: "Safe blood glucose control in critically ill patients requires a validated protocol, with accurate and frequent blood glucose measurements and avoidance of insulin boluses."

items.question: A patient remains ventilated 4 weeks with wasting and immune dysfunction. Endocrine context?
options: Prolonged illness endocrine profile may be maladaptive and contribute to catabolism
All endocrine changes normalize by day 7 in every ICU patient
Acute adaptive changes persist unchanged indefinitely without consequence
Neuroendocrine axes are unaffected by ICU length of stay
items.explanation: Prolonged ICU dependence brings a maladaptive endocrine profile contributing to hypercatabolism and immune suppression.
items.reference: Conclusion: "In patients who do not recover swiftly and remain dependent on intensive care for a prolonged period of time, a different endocrine profile develops, which may be maladaptive by contributing to the hypercatabolic phenotype and immune suppression associated with this condition."

items.question: Acute critical illness: cortisol remains high while ACTH is low. Primary explanation?
options: Suppressed cortisol breakdown plus altered binding proteins increasing free cortisol
Unlimited ACTH hypersecretion from pituitary adenoma
Complete adrenal cortex atrophy within hours
Isolated decrease in CRH with absent cortisol production
items.explanation: ACTH-cortisol dissociation after the hyperacute phase reflects suppressed cortisol metabolism and altered CBG/albumin binding increasing free cortisol.
items.reference: Alterations in the Hypothalamic-Pituitary-Adrenal Axis: "Thereafter, ACTH concentrations rapidly decline to low levels, while cortisol concentrations remain persistently elevated."

items.statement: Acute critical illness is characterized by low thyroid hormone and IGF-1 and high cortisol.
items.explanation: KEY POINTS explicitly describe this acute-phase endocrine pattern as presumably adaptive.
items.reference: KEY POINTS: "Acute critical illness is characterized by low levels of thyroid hormone and insulin-like growth factor 1, and high levels of cortisol, which is presumably adaptive and mainly brought about by peripheral alterations in hormone metabolism."

items.statement: Interventional studies supplementing thyroid hormone in the acute phase of critical illness have shown mortality benefit.
items.explanation: Interventional thyroid hormone supplementation in acute critical illness has not shown outcome benefit.
items.reference: Alterations in the Thyroid Axis: "Interventional studies supplementing thyroid hormone in the acute phase of critical illness have not shown benefit on outcomes."

items.statement: The cosyntropin stimulation test identifies patients with septic shock who benefit from glucocorticoid treatment.
items.explanation: Cosyntropin stimulation does not identify glucocorticoid-responsive patients; large RCTs showed no mortality benefit in low responders.
items.reference: Alterations in the Hypothalamic-Pituitary-Adrenal Axis: "The cosyntropin stimulation test does not identify patients who would benefit from treatment with glucocorticoids."

items.statement: High-dose GH treatment initiated after 5 to 7 days in expected long-stay critically ill patients increased mortality in RCTs.
items.explanation: Two RCTs showed significantly increased mortality with high-dose GH in prolonged critically ill patients.
items.reference: Alterations in the Somatotropic Axis: "two randomized controlled trials (RCTs) revealed that high-dose GH treatment, initiated after 5 to 7 days in expected long-stay critically ill patients, significantly increased mortality, which may be explained by GH overdosing in a state of recovered GH responsiveness."

items.statement: The NICE-SUGAR RCT showed harm from tight glucose control compared with maintaining blood glucose below 180 mg/dL.
items.explanation: NICE-SUGAR demonstrated harm with intensive versus conventional glucose control targeting <180 mg/dL.
items.reference: Insulin Resistance and Stress Hyperglycemia: "the NICE-SUGAR RCT subsequently showed harm by tight glucose control compared with maintaining blood glucose below 180 mg/dL."

items.statement: Insulin boluses are preferred over continuous infusion to rapidly reach glucose targets in critically ill patients.
items.explanation: Continuous IV insulin without boluses is preferred to avoid hypoglycemia and glucose fluctuations linked to poor outcomes.
items.reference: Insulin Resistance and Stress Hyperglycemia: "Insulin is preferably administered through continuous intravenous insulin infusion while avoiding boluses, to slowly progress toward blood glucose target and to avoid hypoglycemia and large glucose fluctuations, because both have been associated with poor outcomes."

items.statement: Large vitamin D supplementation RCTs in critically ill patients showed no clinical endpoint benefit despite raising 25OHD or active hormone.
items.explanation: High-dose vitamin D RCTs increased metabolite levels without clinical benefit in deficient critically ill patients.
items.reference: Alterations in Vitamin D: "Administration of much higher doses of vitamin  $ D_3 $ in large RCTs powered for clinical endpoints did significantly increase 25OHD or 1,25(OH) $ _2 $D in critically ill patients with baseline vitamin D deficiency, although there was no benefit on clinical endpoints."

items.statement: Recent evidence supports generalized glucocorticoid resistance as the uniform explanation for steroid response in all tissues during septic shock.
items.explanation: Tissue-specific glucocorticoid availability and action argue against generalized glucocorticoid resistance in sepsis.
items.reference: Alterations in the Hypothalamic-Pituitary-Adrenal Axis: "Observations documenting tissue-specific alterations in local glucocorticoid availability and action, as well as differential responses associated with increased glucocorticoid availability in human critically ill patients and in a septic mouse model, argue against generalized glucocorticoid resistance in sepsis."

items.statement: Fasting during critical illness evokes thyroid hormone metabolic changes thought to reflect an adaptive response preventing hypercatabolism.
items.explanation: Fasting produces similar thyroid metabolic alterations considered adaptive against hypercatabolism.
items.reference: Alterations in the Thyroid Axis: "Of note, fasting evokes similar alterations in thyroid hormone metabolism and is thought to reflect an adaptive response preventing hypercatabolism."

items.statement: Dopamine administration centrally suppresses the somatotropic axis in critically ill patients.
items.explanation: Dopamine used as vasoactive therapy centrally suppresses the somatotropic axis.
items.reference: Alterations in the Somatotropic Axis: "The administration of dopamine treatment, commonly used as vasoactive strategy in the past, centrally suppresses the somatotropic axis."

items.statement: A considerable proportion of critically ill patients may have previously unknown diabetes detectable by elevated admission HbA1c.
items.explanation: Recent studies show many ICU patients have antecedent diabetes by elevated admission HbA1c.
items.reference: Insulin Resistance and Stress Hyperglycemia: "Moreover, recent studies have shown that a considerable amount of critically ill patients may have previously unknown antecedent diabetes mellitus, as derived from elevated HbA $ _{1c} $ levels on ICU admission."

items.statement: Illness-induced endocrine alterations often impede diagnosis of preexisting endocrine disease in the ICU.
items.explanation: The conclusion states illness-induced alterations often impede diagnosis of preexisting endocrine disease.
items.reference: Conclusion: "The illness-induced alterations often impede the diagnosis of preexisting endocrine disease."

items.statement: The ideal blood glucose target in critically ill patients not receiving early parenteral nutrition has been definitively established.
items.explanation: KEY POINTS state the ideal glucose target without early parenteral nutrition remains unclear.
items.reference: KEY POINTS: "The ideal blood glucose target in critically ill patients not receiving early parenteral nutrition remains unclear."

items.assertion: Assertion: Low T3 in acute critical illness reflects euthyroid sick syndrome.
items.reason: Reason: Systemic inflammation and fasting accompanying severe illness trigger altered peripheral thyroid hormone metabolism.
items.explanation: Both true and linked—inflammation and fasting drive the low T3 biochemical constellation of nonthyroidal illness.
items.reference: Alterations in the Thyroid Axis: "Triggers of this biochemical constellation are systemic inflammation and fasting accompanying"

items.assertion: Assertion: Elevated cortisol in critical illness does not exclude adrenal insufficiency.
items.reason: Reason: High cortisol in critical illness always proves intact adrenal reserve.
items.explanation: Assertion true; reason false—elevated cortisol reflects peripheral adaptations and random cortisol is hard to interpret.
items.reference: Alterations in the Hypothalamic-Pituitary-Adrenal Axis: "Hence, random cortisol measurements can be difficult to interpret because elevated cortisol concentrations do not exclude adrenal insufficiency."

items.assertion: Assertion: High-dose GH increased mortality in long-stay critically ill patients.
items.reason: Reason: Peripheral GH responsiveness may recover, making GH overdosing hazardous.
items.explanation: Both true and linked—recovered responsiveness may explain mortality from high-dose GH in prolonged illness.
items.reference: Alterations in the Somatotropic Axis: "significantly increased mortality, which may be explained by GH overdosing in a state of recovered GH responsiveness."

items.assertion: Assertion: Tight glucose control showed benefit in Leuven RCTs.
items.reason: Reason: Leuven trials used early parenteral nutrition, a context in which tight control benefit was demonstrated.
items.explanation: Both true and linked—benefit of tight control was tied to early parenteral nutrition feeding strategy.
items.reference: Insulin Resistance and Stress Hyperglycemia: "In this context, a benefit of tight glucose control was only demonstrated in a context of early parenteral nutrition supplementing insufficient enteral nutrition, $ ^{10-12,61} $"

items.assertion: Assertion: Hypoglycemia and large glucose fluctuations are associated with poor outcomes in critical illness.
items.reason: Reason: Insulin boluses are the preferred method to minimize glucose variability in ICU.
items.explanation: Assertion true; reason false—boluses should be avoided; continuous infusion is preferred.
items.reference: Insulin Resistance and Stress Hyperglycemia: "Insulin is preferably administered through continuous intravenous insulin infusion while avoiding boluses, to slowly progress toward blood glucose target and to avoid hypoglycemia and large glucose fluctuations, because both have been associated with poor outcomes."

items.assertion: Assertion: Low vitamin D in acute critical illness is probably adaptive.
items.reason: Reason: All critically ill patients require high-dose vitamin D3 supplementation on ICU admission.
items.explanation: Assertion true; reason false—acute low vitamin D does not require pharmacologic correction beyond maintenance dose.
items.reference: Alterations in Vitamin D: "Altogether, current evidence suggests that low vitamin D concentrations are adaptive during acute critical illness and do not require pharmacologic correction on top of a normal maintenance dose."

items.assertion: Assertion: ACTH declines while cortisol stays elevated after acute critical stress.
items.reason: Reason: Cortisol breakdown is suppressed after the hyperacute phase.
items.explanation: Both true and linked—suppressed cortisol metabolism helps explain persistent hypercortisolemia with low ACTH.
items.reference: Alterations in the Hypothalamic-Pituitary-Adrenal Axis: "The persistent hypercortisolemia in the presence of suppressed ACTH (ACTH-cortisol dissociation) after the hyperacute phase is explained by suppressed breakdown of cortisol."

items.assertion: Assertion: Thyroid hormone should be supplemented in all acute critically ill patients with low T3.
items.reason: Reason: Acute thyroid alterations are assumed adaptive and supplementation trials showed no outcome benefit.
items.explanation: Assertion false; reason true—acute low T3 is adaptive and supplementation has not helped outcomes.
items.reference: Alterations in the Thyroid Axis: "In the acute phase of critical illness, these alterations are assumed to be adaptive. $ ^{2} $ Interventional studies supplementing thyroid hormone in the acute phase of critical illness have not shown benefit on outcomes."

items.assertion: Assertion: Prolonged critical illness causes central suppression of multiple pituitary axes.
items.reason: Reason: Central suppression improves rehabilitation without need for further study.
items.explanation: Assertion true; reason false—central suppression may impair rehabilitation and treatment needs adequately powered trials.
items.reference: KEY POINTS: "In patients with prolonged critical illness, a central suppression of the somatotropic, thyrotropic, and adrenocorticotropic axes progressively develops, which may impair rehabilitation, although treatment indications and regimens remain to be studied in adequately powered trials."

items.assertion: Assertion: Acute critical illness features high GH with low IGF-1.
items.reason: Reason: GH secretion decreases to undetectable levels in the acute phase.
items.explanation: Assertion true; reason false—acute illness increases GH secretion with peripheral resistance lowering IGF-1.
items.reference: Alterations in the Somatotropic Axis: "In the acute phase of critical illness, growth hormone (GH) secretion increases, with increased pulse frequency and amplitude and elevated trough concentrations."

items.assertion: Assertion: Hydrocortisone RCTs in septic shock often shortened vasopressor duration.
items.reason: Reason: All hydrocortisone RCTs uniformly reduced mortality by 50%.
items.explanation: Both true but not causally linked—vasopressor weaning is common but mortality benefit is inconsistent.
items.reference: Alterations in the Hypothalamic-Pituitary-Adrenal Axis: "Although most RCTs demonstrated faster weaning of vasopressor support, which could be expected based on the well-known blood pressure-augmenting effects of steroids, only one RCT found significant mortality benefit."

items.assertion: Assertion: Selected endocrine abnormalities may persist for years after critical illness.
items.reason: Reason: All neuroendocrine axes fully normalize within 48 hours of ICU discharge in every survivor.
items.explanation: Assertion true per conclusion; reason false—some abnormalities may persist years after critical illness.
items.reference: Conclusion: "In patients surviving critical illness, selected endocrine abnormalities may persist for years after randomization."
